babyDevelopment: The baby is considered 'full term.' The brain and lungs are fully mature. The baby is continuing to accumulate fat to help regulate body temperature after birth. Movement might feel slower due to the cramped space, but should still be frequent.
bodyChanges: You may experience 'bloody show'—a small amount of blood-tinged mucus as your cervix thins and opens. Contractions may become more frequent and intense. You might feel very restless and ready to meet your baby.
tips: Finalize any remaining tasks for baby's arrival
Ensure you have plenty of rest
Monitor baby's movement several times a day without fail

do: Keep up with gentle daily movement
Follow your doctor's instructions for when to go to the hospital
dont: Travel more than 30 minutes from your birthing center
Panic if you're slightly past your due date

travelAdvisory.note: Stay at home and prepare for labor.

warningSignsToWatch: Sudden severe pain in upper abdomen
milestoneDescription: You've reached full term! Your baby is ready for the world.
